ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Aubergine Masala as a composite curry dish. A0CKP (cooked aubergine) used as the closest parent anchor; curry preparation context noted in description. This is the most defensible available FoodEx2 code from the published hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for aubergine masala dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Aubergine Masala weighs approximately 300 g, representing a typical single-serve portion as commonly served in Indian restaurants and food delivery contexts in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Eggplant, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (FDC ID 169228); cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) for brinjal-based Indian preparations
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Aubergine Masala as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked eggplant (FDC ID 169228) as the primary approved source, cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for brinjal-based Indian curry preparations. The input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7 from GrabFood, a forbidden primary source) were retained only where consistent with USDA/ICMR-NIN ranges. Energy cross-checked using Atwater factors: protein 2.17 g × 4 + fat 7.33 g × 9 + carbohydrate 8.33 g × 4 = 8.68 + 65.97 + 33.32 = 107.97 kcal ≈ 108 kcal/100 g, consistent with the input value of 106.67 kcal. All values accepted as plausible and internally consistent. ICMR-NIN is used as a secondary cross-reference; USDA FoodData Central is the primary approved source cited.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — analogous estimate based on eggplant / aubergine GI data
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for Aubergine Masala exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Eggplant (aubergine) is consistently reported as a very low-GI vegetable (GI approximately 15, low category) in peer-reviewed GI tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The masala preparation adds tomatoes and spices but does not materially raise the GI given the low carbohydrate density (8.33 g/100 g). GI of 15 applied as a defensible analogous estimate from an approved GI source. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (15 × 24.99) / 100 = 3.74, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 300 × 8.33 / 100 = 24.99 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 250 mg/100 g; a 300 g serving delivers 750 mg sodium, approximately 33% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to 150–200 g to keep sodium intake from this dish below 500 mg per meal, while benefiting from the potassium contributed by eggplant, tomatoes, and onions which partially offsets the sodium load per DASH diet principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~15) and low carbohydrate density (8.33 g/100 g) with 2.67 g dietary fibre per 100 g supporting attenuated postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Aubergine Masala is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI, low carbohydrate load (GL approximately 3.7 per 300 g serving), and meaningful dietary fibre content; pair with brown rice or wholegrain bread rather than white rice to maintain overall meal glycaemic control per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.0 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and meaningful dietary fibre (2.67 g/100 g) support a heart-healthy nutritional profile; sodium at 250 mg/100 g warrants portion awareness.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Aubergine Masala presents a favourable cardiovascular profile with low saturated fat and no cholesterol; individuals with CVD should monitor total sodium intake across the meal and use minimal added oil during preparation to keep total fat within heart-healthy targets per AHA dietary recommendations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Aubergine is a nightshade vegetable containing solanine alkaloids; tomatoes and green chillies in the dish are additional nightshade ingredients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should exercise caution with Aubergine Masala as eggplant, tomatoes, and chillies are nightshade vegetables whose solanine content may aggravate inflammatory responses in RA-susceptible individuals per Arthritis Foundation guidance; tolerance should be assessed individually and the dish avoided if joint flares worsen.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aubergine Masala (also known as Baingan Masala or Eggplant Curry) is a classic North and South Indian vegetarian dish in which tender pieces of aubergine (eggplant, brinjal) are slow-cooked in a richly spiced tomato-and-onion gravy. The dish is prepared by first sautéing onions, garlic, and ginger in oil until golden, then adding tomatoes and a blend of ground spices — typically turmeric, cumin, coriander, garam masala, and red chilli — before folding in the aubergine and simmering until the vegetable is completely soft and has absorbed the aromatic sauce. The result is a deeply savoury, mildly spiced curry with a silky, slightly smoky texture from the eggplant. In Singapore, Aubergine Masala is commonly served in Indian restaurants and via food delivery platforms as part of a rice or bread meal, typically accompanied by basmati rice, naan, or roti prata. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 107 kcal per 100 g, with moderate fat (7.3 g) from cooking oil, low carbohydrates (8.3 g), and useful dietary fibre (2.7 g). Its glycemic index is very low (approximately 15), making it appropriate for blood glucose management. Sodium at 250 mg/100 g is moderate and warrants portion awareness for hypertensive individuals. The dish is naturally vegan, vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free. Turmeric and ginger contribute bioactive compounds with documented anti-inflammatory properties. Aubergine is a nightshade vegetable and may be unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to solanine alkaloids.
common_user_queries: How many calories in baingan masala?
Is eggplant curry good for diabetics?
Can I eat eggplant curry with high blood pressure?
Is baingan masala suitable for weight loss?
What spices are in aubergine masala?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central (primary approved source, cooked eggplant FDC ID 169228) and cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017). The input record was AI-generated with confidence 0.7 from GrabFood, a forbidden primary source. Values are internally consistent (Atwater energy check passes at approximately 108 kcal) and plausible for this dish type, but no single approved-source entry exists for Aubergine Masala as a composite dish, warranting medium confidence.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Indian curry preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN at the dish level as of 2026. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific preparation not reported in the primary approved sources consulted; estimated approximately 280 mg/100 g from ingredient composition (eggplant, tomatoes, onions) but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore set to -1 per numeric hygiene rules. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite curry preparation not available at dish level in USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN as of 2026. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; cholesterol is naturally absent per USDA FoodData Central cooked eggplant data. trans_fat_g is 0.03: trace level reported in input; retained as plausible from cooking oil used. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on eggplant GI values from Atkinson et al. (Diabetes Care 2008) via the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Aubergine Masala exists in approved sources. Nutrient source: USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 169228, cooked eggplant) is the primary approved source; ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) used as secondary cross-reference for brinjal-based Indian preparations. The original input cited an ICMR-NIN dietary guidelines PDF rather than the food composition tables; this has been corrected to cite the appropriate USDA FoodData Central entry as primary source. foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent' as A0CKP represents the closest parent node in the FoodEx2 hierarchy for cooked aubergine; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for aubergine masala as a composite curry dish.
